question: Explain the concept of granularity of data items in DBMS. What are the different levels of granularity?
expectedPoints: Definition of granularity as the size of data items that can be locked
Levels from coarse to fine: Database → File → Page → Record → Field
Explanation that coarse granularity means large data items, fine granularity means small items
Trade-off between concurrency and overhead

question: What are the six lock modes in Multiple Granularity Locking? Explain any two intent locks with their purpose.
expectedPoints: List six modes: IS, IX, S, SIX, X, NL
IS (Intent Shared): indicates intention to read lower-level items
IX (Intent Exclusive): indicates intention to modify lower-level items
Purpose: efficient lock management in hierarchical structure

question: Explain the lock compatibility matrix for Multiple Granularity Locking with an example.
expectedPoints: Matrix shows which locks can be held simultaneously
IS compatible with IS, S, IX
IX compatible with IS, IX
S compatible only with IS, S, IX
X compatible only with NL
SIX compatible only with IS
Example: If transaction A holds S lock, transaction B can request IS but not X lock

question: What is lock escalation? When and why does it occur?
expectedPoints: Definition: conversion of fine-grained locks to coarse-grained locks
Triggered when number of locks exceeds threshold
Reasons: reduces lock management overhead, saves memory
Trade-off: may reduce concurrency but improves performance

question: Compare coarse granularity locking and fine granularity locking with respect to concurrency and overhead.
expectedPoints: Coarse: low overhead, less memory, fewer operations, but lower concurrency
Fine: high concurrency, more locks, higher memory and CPU overhead
Fine suitable for transactions accessing few items
Coarse suitable for transactions accessing many items

question: Why are intent locks necessary in Multiple Granularity Locking? Explain with an example.
expectedPoints: Purpose: indicate presence of locks at lower levels in hierarchy
Avoids scanning entire tree to check compatibility
Example: If a transaction holds IX lock on database, it indicates it may modify records; other transactions can quickly determine compatibility without checking each record
Improves lock management efficiency

question: Draw and explain the lock compatibility matrix for Multiple Granularity Locking. For each lock mode, show which locks it is compatible with.
expectedPoints: Draw 6x6 matrix with lock modes as rows and columns
Explain compatibility entries: IS compatible with IS, S, IX; IX compatible with IS, IX; S compatible with IS, S, IX; SIX compatible with IS only; X compatible with NL only; NL compatible with all
Explain why certain incompatibilities exist (e.g., X needs exclusive access)
Provide at least 2 examples showing lock grant/denial scenarios

question: Explain the Multiple Granularity Locking protocol with special reference to the lock acquisition and release procedure. How does it ensure serializability?
expectedPoints: Explain hierarchy of locks (database, file, page, record)
Lock acquisition: top-down manner (root to leaf)
Lock release: bottom-up manner (leaf to root)
Rules: Before acquiring S or X lock on a node, must have IS or IX on parent; Before acquiring X on node, must have IX or SIX on parent
Two-phase locking: growing phase (acquire locks) and shrinking phase (release locks)
Ensures serializability by preventing dirty reads and unrepeatable reads

question: A transaction wants to modify a specific record in a file while simultaneously reading the entire file. Explain the locking protocol followed. What locks will be acquired and at what levels?
expectedPoints: Use SIX lock on file (S component for reading entire file, IX component for intention to modify)
Use X lock on specific record for modification
Explain why SIX is appropriate: allows reading all records while indicating intent to modify descendants
Explain lock hierarchy: SIX on file node, X on record leaf
Explain compatibility: Other transactions can read file (S lock), but cannot modify any record (X lock prevents)

question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of coarse granularity and fine granularity locking. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?
expectedPoints: Coarse advantages: Less memory, lower overhead, fewer lock operations, faster
Coarse disadvantages: Lower concurrency, more lock conflicts
Fine advantages: Higher concurrency, fewer conflicts, better for few-item access
Fine disadvantages: High overhead, more memory, complex management
Choose coarse for: batch operations, full-table scans, few large transactions
Choose fine for: OLTP, transactions accessing few rows, high concurrency requirements
Lock escalation as a dynamic adaptation technique
